request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"ubiome::biotech","name":"ubiome","one_liner":"US-based microbiome sequencing startup that sold mail-in gut-testing kits with a companion app on a subscription model, ultimately shut down after FBI investigation into Medicare billing fraud and SEC charges against its founders.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both use a consumer-facing subscription model with a digital health app component. However, uBiome's core revenue engine shifted heavily toward insurance/Medicare billing for clinical tests rather than pure direct-to-consumer subscriptions, while the new pitch is a clean monthly subscription bundling symptom tracking with telehealth — no lab testing or insurance billing involved."},"market":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both target consumer health with an app layer aimed at individuals managing their own health data. uBiome focused on microbiome curiosity and clinical diagnostics in the US; the new pitch targets chronic-illness patients in Germany and France — a distinct condition-specific, geographically separate market with different regulatory regimes (EU MDR, national health reimbursement schemes)."},"gtm":{"score":3,"rationale":"uBiome used crowdfunding, Y Combinator, and VC backing to reach US consumers and later pushed through physician channels for insurance reimbursement. The new pitch targets EU chronic-illness patients likely via direct digital acquisition and telehealth partnerships — no physician-order or insurance-billing channel indicated, and a very different geography."},"stage_scale":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both are early-stage consumer health startups that raised venture funding (uBiome raised $15.5M+ and reached ~$600M valuation before collapse). The new pitch appears pre-scale, similar to where uBiome was in its early subscription-only phase before the clinical pivot."}},"why_similar":"Both companies are consumer-facing digital health startups built around a recurring subscription model and a companion app, targeting individuals who want to take an active role in monitoring their own health data. uBiome's original direct-to-consumer crowdfunded thesis — sell health insights to curious/health-conscious consumers via an app and subscription — mirrors the structural premise of the new pitch. Both also sit at the intersection of health data collection, software, and patient empowerment.","where_diverged":"1. Geography: uBiome operated exclusively in the US and billed US Medicare; the new pitch targets Germany and France, which means EU regulatory frameworks (EU MDR, GDPR, national telemedicine laws) rather than FDA/CLIA. 2. Product type: uBiome sold a physical mail-in lab test kit; the new pitch is a pure software service (symptom tracking app + on-demand telehealth) with no physical product or lab component. 3. Revenue model purity: uBiome's downfall was largely driven by a pivot into insurance reimbursement billing; the new pitch stays entirely within direct consumer subscription, avoiding third-party payer complexity. 4. Disease focus: uBiome was general microbiome curiosity / STI testing; the new pitch is explicitly scoped to chronic-illness patients, a defined and underserved clinical cohort. 5. Telehealth integration: the new pitch bundles on-demand telehealth as a core value driver, which uBiome never offered.","failure_causes":["Insurance/Medicare billing fraud","Repeated unauthorized patient billing","Founder misrepresentation to investors (securities fraud)","Investor capital raised under false pretenses","Absence of credible independent clinical validation","Regulatory and ethics violations (lack of IRB approval at launch, CLIA compliance issues)","Loss of physician and institutional trust triggering FBI investigation"],"lessons_for_input":["Never let a reimbursement or billing channel grow faster than your compliance infrastructure — the moment you touch third-party payers, institute rigorous oversight from day one.","In EU healthtech, validate data privacy (GDPR) and medical device classification (EU MDR) before scaling; regulatory surprise is an existential risk as lethal as fraud.","Anchor clinical credibility early: obtain independent validation of your symptom-tracking algorithms and telehealth protocols before marketing outcomes claims to chronic-illness patients.","Build governance controls (independent board members, audit committee, investor reporting standards) proportional to the trust patients place in you — founder-controlled opacity is a red flag investors and regulators will eventually act on.","Keep the subscription model clean and patient-consented: uBiome's collapse was seeded the moment it began billing without explicit patient consent — make consent, billing transparency, and easy cancellation core product features, not afterthoughts."]}
